name: Acorn
description: Connects local partners and smallholder farmers to carbon markets through agroforestry project support, remote-sensing-enabled MRV, certification, and commercialization.
overview: Acorn is a Rabobank-supported smallholder agroforestry carbon platform that helps local partners and farmers design agroforestry projects, monitor biomass growth, certify ex-post carbon removal units, and sell credits through carbon markets.
representativeProjects: Solidaridad Colombia agroforestry project
Farm Africa Acorn agroforestry project in Embu and Tharaka Nithi, Kenya
FSD Africa / Rabo Foundation launch-phase Acorn projects in Kenya, Nigeria, and Zambia
standards: Plan Vivo
Verra
financingApproach: Acorn sells ex-post carbon removal units generated from measured agroforestry biomass growth. Its public materials describe a revenue-sharing model in which at least 70% of carbon credit revenue flows back to farmers, 15% goes to the local partner, and 15% goes to Acorn for administration and operational costs. The FSD Africa / Rabo Foundation collaboration tested a financing structure in which loans help small-scale farmers transition to agroforestry and are repaid from carbon removal unit proceeds.

businessModel: Smallholder agroforestry carbon platform. Acorn works through local partners to enroll farmers, measure biomass growth, certify ex-post carbon removal units, commercialize credits, and share carbon revenue with farmers and implementation partners. Carbon removals are its primary product and carbon revenue is the model's central financing mechanism.

name: PUR
description: Develops agroforestry, restoration, and insetting programs for companies, often linked to corporate supply chains, Scope 3 emissions strategies, and long-term farmer and landscape resilience.
overview: PUR is a France-headquartered nature-based solutions developer that helps companies build resilient supply chains, develop high-integrity nature-based carbon removal projects, and scale measurable climate and nature impact through locally embedded project teams.
businessModel: Corporate-funded nature-based project development and implementation. PUR works with companies to design, implement, monitor, and scale insetting, agroforestry, restoration, and carbon removal programs that generate supply-chain resilience, Scope 3 emissions outcomes, carbon assets, and wider nature benefits. Carbon is usually an outcome or financing layer within a broader corporate nature program rather than the only product.

financingApproach: PUR's public materials emphasize corporate-funded supply-chain insetting and nature-based solution investments rather than a single project-finance structure. Companies work with PUR to fund interventions inside or around their value chains, including agroforestry and landscape restoration designed to support Scope 3 emissions reductions, carbon removals, biodiversity, water, and farmer livelihood outcomes. In carbon-credit-oriented projects, PUR also works with certification pathways and partners such as ClimeCo, where project funding can be linked to the sale of verified carbon units.

name: re.green
description: Develops large-scale ecological restoration projects with carbon revenue, biodiversity, and native ecosystem recovery at the center of the model.
overview: re.green is a Brazilian ecological landscape restoration company that develops and manages native-forest projects in the Amazon and Atlantic Forest. Its Total Forest Stewardship model combines active restoration, assisted natural regeneration, conservation of forest remnants, science-led land selection and design, technology-enabled monitoring, and local community engagement. The company commercializes verified carbon removals and other forest products and services to make large-scale, biodiverse restoration financially durable.
businessModel: Vertically integrated ecological restoration developer and forest manager. re.green identifies and secures degraded land through acquisitions and partnerships, designs and implements restoration with native species, manages seed and seedling supply through Bioflora and partner nurseries, monitors ecological and carbon outcomes, and sells high-integrity carbon removals through long-term corporate offtakes and public procurement programs. Carbon removals are a primary product and restoration-finance mechanism, complemented in suitable projects by sustainable native-timber and other biological products.
representativeProjects: Ouro Verde, Eunápolis, Bahia — Atlantic Forest restoration and part of re.green's first Verra-certified carbon-credit issuance
Belo Horizonte, Potiraguá, Bahia — Atlantic Forest restoration and part of re.green's first Verra-certified carbon-credit issuance

ProFloresta+ Amazon restoration project — more than 4,800 hectares and approximately 1 million carbon credits contracted for Petrobras over 25 years

financingApproach: re.green blends long-term carbon purchase commitments, project-level debt, corporate restoration contracts, and land partnerships. Microsoft signed two carbon-removal offtakes covering restoration in the Amazon and Atlantic Forest; the first calls for about 3 million tonnes of removals over 15 years, and the second adds nearly 3.5 million tonnes. In 2025, re.green secured R$80 million from BNDES's Climate Fund, backed by a Bradesco guarantee and biodiversity labeling, for restoration across more than 16,000 hectares. In 2026, ProFloresta+ selected re.green to supply Petrobras with 1 million Amazon restoration credits over 25 years, providing long-term contracted demand. Verified credit sales, sustainable native-timber and other forest products, and agreements with corporate clients and landowners form additional parts of the model.

comparison.platformComparison: re.green and Acorn are both carbon-focused developers that coordinate implementation, MRV, certification, and sales, so readers may encounter both as suppliers of nature-based removals. Their operating and financing models differ: re.green vertically integrates land access, native-forest restoration, stewardship, and long-term offtakes for large Brazilian landscapes, while Acorn aggregates smallholder agroforestry through local partners and shares ex-post credit revenue with farmers. The lesson is scale and control: integrated landscape ownership and management versus a distributed farmer platform.

name: Taking Root
description: Builds farmer-centered reforestation and agroforestry carbon programs with technology, local implementation, and long-term monitoring.
overview: Taking Root is a Canadian project developer that designs, implements, and monitors community-based reforestation and agroforestry projects. Its model combines local implementation partners, long-term farmer engagement, proprietary digital monitoring technology, and verified carbon credit generation to finance landscape restoration.
businessModel: Taking Root develops and operates forest restoration and agroforestry carbon projects, working with local partners and smallholder landholders. The company combines project development, digital MRV technology through its FARM-TRACE platform, long-term monitoring, carbon certification, and commercialization of verified carbon credits to fund restoration. Carbon credits are its primary product and financing mechanism.

Carbon revenue supports long-term maintenance, monitoring, and payments to participating landholders while digital MRV improves transparency and efficiency.

comparison.platformComparison: Taking Root and Acorn both use local partners, digital MRV, certification, and credit sales to finance smallholder tree-growing, which makes them close comparators. Taking Root is more vertically integrated around community restoration programs it develops and operates, with FARM-TRACE supporting long-term monitoring; Acorn is a broader aggregation platform through which local partners enroll farmers and share ex-post removal revenue. name: Solidaridad
description: Works with producers, supply chains, and landscapes. In agroforestry carbon, Solidaridad often plays the field implementation role: organizing farmers, delivering training, supporting agroforestry adoption, and connecting smallholders to carbon finance through partners and platforms.
overview: Solidaridad is an international civil society organization that works with smallholder producers and commodity supply chains. In agroforestry carbon, it is most relevant as an implementation partner: helping farmers adopt climate-smart agroforestry systems, access training, and participate in carbon finance programs such as Acorn-linked carbon removal projects.
businessModel: Program implementation and development partnership model. Solidaridad earns and deploys funding through donor, corporate, foundation, and platform partnerships to deliver farmer training, technical assistance, supply-chain sustainability programs, and carbon-market readiness. It connects funders, brands, carbon platforms, producer organizations, and farmers, but does not act as the standard, registry, or corporate buyer. Carbon is not the core product; it is a financing mechanism and farmer incentive within broader supply-chain and rural-development programs.

The model relies on local implementation capacity and farmer training before verified carbon revenue can become meaningful.

name: TechnoServe
description: Provides technical assistance, market-systems support, and regenerative agriculture programming for smallholder farmers and agribusinesses. In agroforestry carbon, TechnoServe is most relevant as an implementation and advisory partner that can help producers adopt practices such as shade trees, soil management, composting, and climate-smart coffee production.
overview: TechnoServe is an international nonprofit that works with enterprising people in the developing world to build competitive farms, businesses, and industries. In the agroforestry carbon field, it is most relevant as an implementation and technical assistance partner: helping smallholder farmers and agribusinesses adopt regenerative practices, improve productivity and income, and connect climate-smart agriculture to supply-chain and finance strategies.

name: Catholic Relief Services
description: International NGO implementing agriculture, livelihoods, climate resilience, and landscape restoration programs. In agroforestry carbon, CRS is most relevant as a field implementation and resilience partner where carbon finance can support broader restoration, adaptation, food security, and farmer livelihood objectives.
overview: Catholic Relief Services (CRS) is the official international humanitarian agency of the Catholic community in the United States. Its fiscal 2024 annual report describes work in 134 countries with 1,978 local partners, including 113 agriculture projects in 47 countries. CRS implements nearly all of its programming with local organizations and combines agriculture, livelihoods, humanitarian response, institutional strengthening, and climate resilience. In agroforestry carbon, CRS is most relevant as a community-centered implementation partner that can organize restoration, farmer support, local governance, and safeguards while linking carbon finance to adaptation, food security, and durable livelihood outcomes.
businessModel: CRS is a nonprofit implementer funded through public awards, institutional donors, philanthropy, private contributions, and project-specific climate and development finance. It designs and manages multi-year programs while delivering nearly all programming with local organizations, including civil-society, Church, government, farmer, and community institutions. Its agriculture model combines resilient production, land restoration, farmer organization, market and financial-service links, and local capacity strengthening. Carbon is not a standalone CRS product: CRS positions itself as a value-aligned implementer for project developers and investors, using carbon finance to support community-led agroforestry, watershed restoration, and drylands regreening where integrity and community benefits are credible.

financingApproach: CRS blends public-donor awards, philanthropy, private contributions, and climate-program funding for agriculture, livelihoods, restoration, and resilience. It also helps governments and local civil society pursue national and international climate finance. Its carbon strategy uses investment and future carbon-market value to fund community-led nature-based solutions: Restore Africa, for example, expanded through a US$150 million investment from Climate Asset Management after a Kenya pilot with Temasek. CRS also uses other mechanisms where appropriate, including payments for environmental services through water funds, debt-for-nature finance, and catalytic impact-investment instruments. Carbon revenue is therefore one financing layer within a broader development and resilience model, not CRS's core product.

comparison.implementationComparison: CRS and TechnoServe can both train farmers, work through local partners, and implement regenerative agriculture. The practical distinction is emphasis. CRS is the more natural fit when a program begins with vulnerable communities, food security, humanitarian-development links, local-institution strengthening, landscape restoration, and adaptation; it can then layer carbon or other climate finance onto those outcomes. TechnoServe is the more natural fit when the implementation engine is business and markets: improving farm or agribusiness competitiveness, strengthening a crop value chain, connecting producers to buyers and finance, and making regenerative practices commercially durable. This is not a difference in capability so much as the organizing logic of the engagement: community resilience and multi-sector development for CRS, versus enterprise performance and inclusive market systems for TechnoServe.

name: Verra
description: Develops and manages standards, methodologies, validation and verification rules, and registry infrastructure for voluntary carbon and sustainable development markets. In agroforestry carbon, Verra matters because many nature-based projects use its VCS Program, AFOLU methodologies, CCB Standards, and registry infrastructure to issue and track credits.
overview: Verra is a nonprofit standards body that develops and manages programs for climate action and sustainable development, including the Verified Carbon Standard (VCS), Climate, Community & Biodiversity Standards (CCB), Sustainable Development Verified Impact Standard (SD VISta), and registry infrastructure. For agroforestry carbon, Verra is most relevant as market infrastructure: it sets crediting rules, approves methodologies, oversees validation and verification requirements, and provides registry systems that allow verified units to be issued, transferred, and retired.
businessModel: Standards and registry infrastructure. Verra does not develop agroforestry projects or finance farmer implementation directly. Its role is to operate standards, methodologies, validation and verification processes, and registry systems that allow eligible projects to generate verified credits and related claims. Project developers and proponents use Verra programs to structure projects, undergo third-party validation and verification, and issue units that can be sold or retired in voluntary markets.

financingApproach: Verra is not a project financier. Its financing relevance is indirect: by defining standards, methodologies, registry procedures, and validation/verification requirements, it creates infrastructure that allows project proponents to issue verified units and access carbon-market revenue. For agroforestry and other AFOLU projects, the relevant financing pathway typically runs from project developer or sponsor to validation, verification, issuance, sale, and retirement of credits, rather than through Verra as a lender or investor.

name: Plan Vivo
description: Focuses on community-led land-use and ecosystem service projects with strong emphasis on local participation and benefit sharing.
overview: Plan Vivo Foundation is an Edinburgh-based NGO and registered Scottish charity that operates certification systems for high-integrity community-led climate and nature projects. Its PV Climate carbon standard focuses on community-led forestry, agroforestry, ecosystem restoration, avoided deforestation, and other land-use projects that combine verified climate benefits with livelihood and ecosystem outcomes.
businessModel: Community-centered carbon and nature certification infrastructure. Plan Vivo does not develop agroforestry projects or finance farmer implementation directly. It sets standards and requirements, reviews project documentation, approves methodologies, oversees validation and verification, operates registry infrastructure, and certifies Plan Vivo Certificates for eligible community-led projects. Its distinctive role is to keep local people and benefit sharing at the center of land-use carbon finance.

Certified PV Climate projects can issue and sell Plan Vivo Certificates, with income from certificate sales used to finance project activities and community benefits. name: Pachama
description: Uses remote sensing, software, and data tools to assess and monitor forest carbon projects and connect buyers with nature-based credits.
overview: Pachama developed a technology platform for forest carbon that uses satellite data, artificial intelligence, and remote sensing to support land screening, project evaluation, monitoring, reporting, and verification (MRV), and access to forest conservation and restoration credits. Its marketplace included Pachama Verified projects, which were existing projects vetted through Pachama's evaluation process, and Pachama Originals, which supported new reforestation projects. Carbon Direct acquired Pachama in November 2025 and is integrating Pachama's forest-carbon digital MRV platform and data capabilities into its science-based carbon management services.

financingApproach: Pachama served corporate buyers and project developers through technology-enabled project evaluation, portfolio sourcing, marketplace access, and monitoring. Pachama Verified provided access to existing vetted forest projects, while Pachama Originals sought catalytic and upfront corporate investment for new reforestation projects. Public examples include Shopify and Mercado Libre funding reforestation in Brazil and a $12 million Lower Mississippi River Valley reforestation collaboration with Ducks Unlimited and PERENfra. Following the November 2025 acquisition, Pachama's technology is being integrated into Carbon Direct's carbon management and project diligence offerings.

platformComparison: Pachama and Acorn are both described as forest-carbon platforms and both connect projects with corporate demand, which can obscure their different roles. Pachama primarily sold technology, project diligence, monitoring, and market access, helping buyers evaluate projects and helping developers attract outside funding; it did not aggregate farmers or perform field implementation. Acorn develops smallholder agroforestry programs through local partners and commercializes the resulting removals under a revenue-sharing model. The lesson is enabling technology and diligence versus carbon-project development and farmer aggregation.

name: Open Forest Protocol
description: Provides digital monitoring, reporting, and verification infrastructure intended to make forest and restoration projects more transparent and accessible.
overview: Open Forest Protocol (OFP) provides open digital infrastructure for forest and agroforestry monitoring, verification, carbon-credit issuance, and public disclosure. Project operators collect georeferenced field data with an offline-capable mobile app and manage projects through a dashboard; independent validator organizations review monitoring submissions, while project and credit information is displayed through the public Atlas and recorded using blockchain infrastructure. OFP also operates its own fully digital carbon standard, initially for afforestation, reforestation, and revegetation (ARR). It is infrastructure for project operators, validators, buyers, and builders rather than an organization that plants or manages forests on their behalf.
businessModel: Digital MRV, registry, and carbon-standard infrastructure for forest project operators. OFP supplies project registration, field-data collection, monitoring workflows, distributed validation, credit issuance, and public traceability from project inception through retirement. Its website says eligible projects can access the end-to-end platform with no up-front cost and that projects as small as 0.3 hectares may apply. The project operator remains responsible for land rights, project design, implementation, monitoring submissions, and compliance; OFP provides the digital and standards layer. Public materials reviewed do not disclose a complete fee or revenue schedule, so the profile does not infer one.
representativeProjects: ReverdeC, Colombia — Celsia's participatory ecological-restoration program generated OFP's first Open Carbon Credits in January 2024 from pilot plots in Valle del Cauca

Gorké Forest, Côte d'Ivoire — OFP agreed with the Ministry of Water and Forests and local developer Agro-Map to provide MRV infrastructure for a 5,067-hectare restoration initiative managed on the ground by Agro-Map with local communities
Fundo Exuberante, Peru — INARSAC's Lush-supported agroforestry and regeneration project in Madre de Dios uses OFP tools for field monitoring, remote-sensing integration, and impact reporting
Amrit Mahal Community Forest, India — a community-led restoration initiative using OFP digital MRV to document planting and seek carbon finance
OFP Atlas — public explorer showing registered projects, monitoring records, validation activity, and carbon data
partners: Government of Côte d'Ivoire and Agro-Map
Independent validator organizations and forest project operators
standards: Open Forest Protocol Afforestation / Reforestation / Revegetation (ARR) Carbon Standard and Methodology, version 1.0
OFP is a carbon standard and digital registry framework, not a verifier under Verra or another third-party carbon program
The ARR methodology uses ex-post crediting after monitoring and validation, a protocol-wide buffer pool for reversal risk, and public project and credit records
The ARR methodology draws on IPCC land-use guidance and Clean Development Mechanism afforestation/reforestation tools
financingApproach: OFP seeks to reduce the cost and administrative barriers between restoration projects and carbon finance by offering registration, monitoring, validation, issuance, and traceability in one digital system with no up-front platform cost for eligible project developers. Credits are issued ex post after monitoring data has been validated, and issued credits go to the project operator's wallet; OFP therefore enables financing and market access but does not itself finance or implement the underlying restoration. At the company level, Open Forest AG announced a 2024 pre-seed round led by Übermorgen Ventures with participation from Backbone Ventures, GS Futures, Bloom Foundation, NEAR Foundation, Fondation Valery, and other supporters. Public materials reviewed do not provide a complete operating-revenue or fee model.

comparison.primaryRevenue: Not fully disclosed; digital MRV, registry, standards, and credit-market infrastructure
comparison.mainOffering: Open digital infrastructure for forest monitoring, distributed validation, carbon-credit issuance, and public traceability
comparison.carbonRole: Enabling infrastructure through a digital standard, MRV workflow, registry, and issuance system
comparison.financing: Project operators receive issued credits and pursue outside buyers; OFP removes the up-front platform charge but does not fund field implementation, while venture and foundation capital has supported the company
comparison.typicalClient: Project operators seeking accessible MRV and issuance, independent validators, and buyers seeking traceable forest-removal credits
comparison.platformComparison: Open Forest Protocol and Pachama both use technology to lower forest-carbon transaction costs, but neither is the field project operator. OFP provides an open, end-to-end standard, data-collection workflow, distributed validation process, registry, and issuance rails; project operators retain the credits and seek outside buyers. Pachama provided proprietary screening, diligence, monitoring, portfolio sourcing, and market access, connecting corporate funding with third-party projects. The lesson is digital certification and issuance infrastructure versus commercial diligence, MRV, and buyer access.

name: Chloris Geospatial
description: Uses satellite-based methods to estimate aboveground biomass and help assess forest carbon stocks and change over time.
overview: Chloris Geospatial is a commercial forest-carbon data and analytics company. Its platform uses Earth-observation data, sensor fusion, LiDAR-informed machine learning, and Bayesian time-series analysis to estimate above-ground live woody biomass stock and change, related carbon emissions and removals, and forest-cover dynamics. Chloris provides annual, wall-to-wall data from 2000 onward at 30-meter resolution and from 2017 onward at 10-meter resolution, with quantified uncertainty for each pixel. Customers use the data for carbon-project screening, development, due diligence, and portfolio monitoring, as well as forest-risk supply-chain accounting and reporting. Chloris supplies the measurement layer; it does not operate a carbon standard or registry, issue credits, sell a project marketplace, or implement forestry activities on the ground.
businessModel: Commercial forest-carbon data and analytics delivered through the Chloris platform, geospatial data downloads, an API, and customized calibration, validation, and analytical services. Chloris serves carbon-project developers, standards and ratings organizations, investors, governments, and companies managing forest-risk supply chains. Its carbon-market product supports ARR, REDD+, and IFM project screening, due diligence, development, and project or portfolio monitoring; its supply-chain offering supports historical baselines, emissions and removals monitoring, and reporting. Chloris also distributes data through partner platforms. Its official carbon-markets page states that direct data access starts at USD 5,000 per year and that partner platforms may offer pay-as-you-go or subscription plans.
representativeProjects: Trove Research integrity assessments — Chloris biomass-dynamics data supports consistent assessment of more than 550 REDD+, ARR, and IFM projects across the voluntary carbon market

Equitable Earth forest carbon standard — Chloris was selected as the coalition's exclusive provider of digital MRV forest-carbon data, including measurement of deforestation, degradation, and carbon-stock improvement
Ecosystem Restoration Standard — Chloris supplies above-ground biomass and forest-growth monitoring within ERS's restoration-project certification process
BeZero Carbon ratings — BeZero integrates Chloris biomass datasets into its multi-source geospatial analysis, while providing ground-plot data to support further validation of Chloris models
Upstream Tech Lens integration — Chloris forest-carbon stock, change, and forest-cover data are made available in a landscape-monitoring workflow for conservation and restoration users
Chloris Biomass Viewer — a public exploration tool for viewing biomass stocks and changes from project sites to national scales before using enterprise data products
standards: Not a carbon standard, registry, or credit issuer
Exclusive digital MRV forest-carbon data provider for the Equitable Earth forest carbon standard
Above-ground biomass and forest-growth monitoring partner for the Ecosystem Restoration Standard
Products are marketed for project development and monitoring across ARR, REDD+, and IFM activities and for jurisdictional programs

Supply-chain products are designed to support accounting and disclosure workflows including the GHG Protocol Land Sector and Removals Standard, SBTi FLAG, CSRD, TNFD, and EUDR; Chloris does not itself certify compliance with these frameworks
financingApproach: Chloris enables carbon finance and corporate climate accounting by selling measurement data and analytics rather than financing projects, issuing credits, or operating a marketplace. Customers can purchase direct platform and API access, volume-based access, customized services, or access through partner platforms; the official carbon-markets page lists direct access starting at USD 5,000 per year. At the company level, Chloris announced an $8.5 million Series A in July 2025 led by Future Energy Ventures, with participation from AXA IM Alts, At One Ventures, Cisco Foundation, Counteract, and Orbia Ventures. Chloris said the funding would support product development, commercial and technical hiring, partnerships, and European expansion.

comparison.customer: Carbon-project developers, standards and ratings organizations, investors, governments, and companies with forest-risk supply chains
comparison.primaryRevenue: Subscriptions and paid access to forest-carbon data, platform and API products, partner distribution, and customized analytical services
comparison.mainOffering: Global satellite-based above-ground biomass stock and change, carbon-flow, and forest-cover data
comparison.carbonRole: Enabling measurement service for project assessment, digital MRV, portfolio analysis, and corporate land-sector accounting
comparison.financing: Customers pay Chloris for data and analytics that support outside investment, diligence, and reporting; Chloris does not finance projects or issue or sell credits
comparison.typicalClient: Organizations that need consistent, spatially explicit biomass and carbon measurements across projects, portfolios, jurisdictions, or supply chains
comparison.platformComparison: Chloris Geospatial is the most measurement-specialized of these three organizations: it focuses on producing satellite-based above-ground biomass and carbon-stock data and tracking changes over time. Pachama has a broader forest-carbon role spanning project diligence, portfolio sourcing and marketplace access, and MRV infrastructure. Open Forest Protocol provides open digital MRV, registry, validation, credit-issuance, and carbon-standard infrastructure. Chloris can provide a measurement input to standards, ratings, project-development, or monitoring workflows, but it does not itself operate the broader diligence marketplace associated with Pachama or the open standard and registry infrastructure operated by Open Forest Protocol. This comparison describes different roles in the forest-carbon ecosystem, not relative quality.

ecosystemFit: Chloris Geospatial specializes in satellite-based biomass and carbon-stock measurement. Pachama has a broader role in forest-carbon diligence, project sourcing, marketplace access, and MRV. Open Forest Protocol provides open digital MRV, registry, and carbon-standard infrastructure. These organizations occupy different, sometimes complementary, roles in the forest-carbon ecosystem; this distinction is not a quality score.

name: Climate Asset Management
description: Climate Asset Management is a specialist investment manager formed by HSBC Asset Management and Pollination. It deploys institutional and corporate capital through a Natural Capital Fund focused on regenerative agriculture, sustainable forestry, and environmental assets in developed markets; a Nature Based Carbon Fund financing restoration and conservation in emerging markets; and a fund for Apple's Restore Fund. Its strategies seek financial returns, carbon credits, and measurable benefits for biodiversity, water, climate resilience, and communities, working with local operators and project developers rather than implementing every activity directly.
overview: Climate Asset Management (CAM) is a London-based natural-capital investment manager established in 2020 by HSBC Asset Management and Pollination. It channels institutional and corporate capital into land-based assets and nature projects through dedicated funds. Its Natural Capital Fund invests in regenerative agriculture, sustainable forestry, and environmental assets in developed markets, while its Nature Based Carbon Fund finances restoration and conservation in emerging markets with verified carbon credits forming part of investor returns. CAM also manages a fund within Apple's Restore Fund. Across its global portfolio, CAM works with local operators and project developers to pursue financial returns alongside biodiversity, water, climate-resilience, and community benefits.
businessModel: Specialist natural-capital fund manager and real-assets investment platform. CAM raises commitments from institutional investors and corporations, selects and structures investments, oversees portfolios, and appoints local operators or project developers to manage assets and deliver projects. Its Natural Capital Fund targets commercial returns from agriculture, forestry, land value, and additional ecosystem-service income such as carbon or conservation. Its Nature Based Carbon Fund provides capital to restoration and conservation projects, particularly at early stages, in exchange for expected high-integrity carbon-credit flows. CAM also manages a dedicated fund for Apple's Restore Fund, where corporate capital is pooled across regenerative agriculture, sustainable forestry, conservation, and restoration investments.
representativeProjects: Restore Africa — a Global EverGreening Alliance-led, farmer-centered restoration programme across Kenya, Ethiopia, Malawi, Tanzania, Uganda, and Zambia; CAM announced a US$150 million financing package and the programme aims to restore about 1.9 million hectares and support 1.5 million smallholder farming families

Iberian regenerative agriculture portfolio — includes a 1,900-hectare aggregation of almond, walnut, and pistachio orchards in Spain and Portugal transitioning to regenerative management with more than 10% of the land reserved for nature
Queensland macadamia project — conversion of about 1,800 hectares of former sugar-cane land into a regeneratively managed native macadamia orchard with native-habitat restoration

Project Olympic Rainforest, Washington State — 68,000 acres transitioning from industrial timber management to FSC-certified climate-smart forestry, with EFM as operating partner and long-term carbon offtakes with Meta and Microsoft

Apple Restore Fund projects in Australia and New Zealand — regenerative macadamia production in Queensland and protection, restoration, and sustainable management of 8,600 hectares of New Zealand forestland
partners: Local operating partners and project developers
standards: Forest Stewardship Council (FSC) certification or management standards for identified forestry assets, including Project Olympic Rainforest and the New Zealand Restore Fund project
Independent third-party verification for carbon credits targeted by the Nature Based Carbon Fund; CAM does not operate a carbon standard or registry
financingApproach:
CAM raises institutional and corporate capital and deploys it through fund structures. At the September 2024 final closes, its two main funds and Apple Restore Fund mandate had raised more than US$1 billion.

The Natural Capital Fund acquires or co-invests in productive land and forestry assets, combining crop or timber income with land value, carbon credits, conservation easements, and other ecosystem services. For Project Olympic Rainforest, co-investment funded the acquisition and long-term Meta and Microsoft offtakes reduced carbon-price and cash-flow risk.

The Nature Based Carbon Fund finances early-stage restoration and conservation against expected carbon-credit generation. Restore Africa used forward credit volumes, with investors receiving generated credits as return.

For the CAM-managed phase of Apple's Restore Fund, Apple committed up to US$200 million, alongside up to US$50 million from TSMC and US$30 million from Murata.
ecosystemFit: Climate Asset Management supplies investment capital to natural-capital assets and nature-based carbon projects, usually through local operators and developers. In agroforestry carbon, it can finance land, regenerative production, restoration, and long-duration project development, but it is not a farmer aggregator, MRV provider, standard, or registry. Its role is to package nature-based opportunities for institutional and corporate investors.

comparison.customer: Institutional investors and corporations seeking natural-capital exposure, financial returns, carbon credits, or nature and climate outcomes
comparison.primaryRevenue: Fund-level returns from productive-land income, asset value, ecosystem services, and carbon credits; public sources do not disclose CAM's fee terms
comparison.mainOffering: Dedicated natural-capital investment funds and corporate mandates
comparison.carbonRole: Investment product and return stream in dedicated strategies; one component of a broader natural-capital platform
comparison.financing: Institutional and corporate investors commit capital to CAM-managed funds and mandates, which finance assets and operators; returns can combine asset cash flows, carbon revenue, and long-term offtakes
comparison.typicalClient: Pension funds, insurers, financial institutions, and corporations such as Apple and its suppliers
comparison.platformComparison: Climate Asset Management and FMO both channel institutional capital into nature-related assets, but they raise and deploy it under different mandates. CAM is a specialist investment manager: investors commit to dedicated natural-capital funds and mandates, which finance assets and operators seeking financial returns from land, commodities, ecosystem services, and carbon. FMO is a development finance institution using its balance sheet, public programs, guarantees, and blended finance to support additionality and market creation across emerging-market sectors. Remember specialist fund management versus catalytic development banking.

name: FMO
description: FMO is the Dutch entrepreneurial development bank, a public-private development finance institution that invests in commercially viable businesses, projects, and financial institutions across developing and emerging markets. It provides long-term loans, direct and fund equity, guarantees, syndications, and capacity development across Agribusiness, Food & Forestry, Energy, and Financial Institutions. In forestry and nature-based solutions, FMO combines its own balance sheet with concessional public programs to address long development periods and risks that can deter commercial investors. Its role is to make impactful private-sector investments bankable, strengthen environmental and social practices, and mobilize additional capital.
overview: FMO is the Dutch entrepreneurial development bank, founded in 1970 as a public-private development finance institution with the Dutch State holding 51 percent of its shares. It supports sustainable private-sector growth in developing and emerging markets through investments in Agribusiness, Food & Forestry, Energy, and Financial Institutions. FMO finances commercially viable companies and projects with debt, equity, guarantees, and mobilized capital, while capacity development and environmental, social, and governance engagement support customers beyond financing. Forestry is a strategic climate area: FMO invests directly, through debt and equity funds, and through blended-finance programs intended to reduce risk and attract commercial investment.
businessModel: Public-private development finance institution investing its own balance sheet and administering public and blended-finance programs. FMO provides medium- and long-term loans, syndicated loans, direct and indirect equity, co-investments, and guarantees to commercially viable private-sector businesses and projects where its financing is intended to be additional to what the market provides. It also mobilizes third-party investors and offers capacity development and ESG support. In forestry, FMO can lend directly, invest in debt or equity funds, and co-invest alongside them, using public concessional resources where risks or long time horizons constrain commercial capital.
representativeProjects: Mobilising Finance for Forests — blended-finance program established by FMO and the UK government in 2021, joined by the Netherlands' Ministry of Foreign Affairs in 2024, to mobilize private investment in tropical forests and sustainable land use

Responsible Commodities Facility, Brazil — US$10 million MFF-funded debt supporting lower-interest seasonal credit for Cerrado soy farmers committed to zero deforestation and no conversion of native vegetation

The Reforestation Fund I — equity commitment to a Timberland Investment Group fund targeting degraded land in Brazil, Uruguay, and Chile for FSC-certified commercial forestry and native-forest restoration
SA Impact Forestry Fund, Paraguay — limited-partner equity investment in a forestry and conservation fund reserving at least 25 percent of managed area for conservation and restoration
Ecua America Teak, Ecuador — approved long-tenor loan for plantation expansion, sawmill upgrades, and machinery at an Arbaro Fund portfolio company
Arbaro Fund — forestry fund investment focused on sustainable plantation projects in emerging markets in Latin America and Sub-Saharan Africa
standards: FMO Sustainability Policy Universe, including its exclusion list and position statements
IFC Performance Standards (2012)
World Bank Group Environmental, Health and Safety Guidelines
United Nations Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights
DFI Enhanced Blended Concessional Finance Principles for Private Sector Projects
Forest Stewardship Council (FSC) certification where specified for individual forestry investments; FMO is not a carbon standard or registry
financingApproach:
FMO deploys capital from its own balance sheet through medium- and long-term loans, direct and fund equity, co-investments, and guarantees. It also arranges syndications and mobilizes investors so customers can access larger or more diversified financing packages.

For higher-risk markets and sectors, FMO blends commercial capital with concessional public funding and risk-sharing from partners including the Dutch and UK governments, the European Commission, and the Green Climate Fund. Public programs can also fund technical assistance and market creation to address barriers before or alongside investment.

In forestry, patient capital addresses long development periods and limited early cash flow. FMO invests directly and through debt- and equity-oriented funds, using programs such as Building Prospects, the Dutch Fund for Climate and Development, and Mobilising Finance for Forests to de-risk projects and demonstrate models that may attract commercial investors.
ecosystemFit: FMO can provide patient, risk-tolerant capital to emerging-market agriculture, forestry, restoration, and nature-based businesses when they meet its development mandate and investment criteria. It may finance companies or funds and mobilize co-investors, but it is not a project developer, farmer aggregator, MRV provider, carbon standard, or registry. Its distinctive role is development finance and market creation across sectors, with forestry forming one part of a broader portfolio.

name: Microsoft
description: Microsoft is a demand-side participant in the carbon-removal market: it buys removals to address residual emissions on the way to becoming carbon negative by 2030 and, by 2050, to counterbalance its historical operational emissions. Its significance to developers comes from the scale, duration, and selectivity of its procurement. By signing forward offtake agreements across forest restoration, improved forest management, soils, biochar, mineralization, and other pathways, Microsoft can give suppliers predictable future revenue while signaling expectations for additionality, lifecycle net negativity, durability, monitoring, safeguards, and remedies if delivery fails.
overview: Microsoft sits between its corporate climate commitments and a global network of carbon-removal suppliers: it purchases and retires future removals but does not develop or certify the underlying projects. Developers care because a large, long-dated purchase commitment can validate demand, establish a future revenue stream, and help a project attract the separate debt or equity needed to build and operate.
representativeProjects: re.green, Brazil — two long-term offtakes totaling nearly 6.5 million tonnes from restoration of 33,000 hectares in the Amazon and Atlantic Forest, illustrating large-scale native-ecosystem restoration procurement

Project Olympic Rainforest, Washington State — up to 700,000 improved-forest-management credits through 2035 from a 68,000-acre Climate Asset Management asset operated by EFM; long-term Microsoft and Meta contracts cover substantially all projected carbon removals

Chestnut Carbon, United States — more than 7 million afforestation, reforestation, and revegetation credits under forward-starting 25-year terms, supporting expansion of restoration across former agricultural land
BTG Pactual Timberland Investment Group, Latin America — up to 8 million nature-based removal credits through 2043 from a $1 billion reforestation and restoration strategy
Living Carbon, Appalachia — 1.4 million Isometric-verified credits from reforesting 25,000 acres of degraded and formerly mined land
neustark, Europe — 27,600 tonnes over six years from mineralizing biogenic carbon dioxide in demolished concrete, demonstrating procurement of smaller-volume, highly durable engineered removal
standards: Microsoft Criteria for High-Quality Carbon Dioxide Removal
Independent scientific review and third-party verification
Verra Verified Carbon Standard (project-specific)
Isometric Standard (project-specific)
Forest Stewardship Council certification (project-specific)
financingApproach:
Microsoft primarily provides demand through carbon-removal purchase agreements rather than lending to, managing, or implementing projects. Its procurement includes delivered-credit purchases and long-term forward offtakes under which a supplier commits to deliver verified removals over future years. Microsoft reported signing agreements for 45 million tonnes with 21 companies in FY25, across both nature-based and engineered pathways.

For a developer, a credible long-term buyer reduces uncertainty about whether future credits will sell and creates more predictable contracted revenue. That revenue visibility can improve bankability and help the developer or asset owner raise separate construction capital, project debt, or equity. Project Olympic Rainforest makes the mechanism concrete: Microsoft and Meta offtakes secured commitments for substantially all projected removals, while Climate Asset Management said the contracts reduced carbon-revenue risk and supported the asset's investment case.

The financing effect is indirect but consequential. Microsoft does not become the project developer merely by signing an offtake, and an offtake is not automatically a loan. Instead, its contractual demand can give capital providers greater confidence that a new restoration, forestry, or engineered-removal facility will have customers after it begins delivering. Portfolio diversification across durability, geography, technology, and delivery timing also manages the buyer's own under-delivery and reversal risks while supporting multiple forms of new supply.
businessModel: Corporate carbon-removal procurement. Microsoft uses corporate funds to buy independently reviewed and verified removals from third-party suppliers, then retires delivered credits against its residual and historical-emissions commitments. It creates demand and sets contractual quality requirements, but it is not the project developer, implementation organization, registry, carbon standard, or fund manager.

comparison.platformComparison: Microsoft and Salesforce are both sophisticated corporate buyers and participate together in the Symbiosis Coalition, but their procurement programs emphasize different functions. Microsoft has built an unusually large, project-specific portfolio of long-term removal offtakes intended to supply its carbon-negative and historical-emissions commitments. Salesforce currently uses a disclosed mix of avoidance and removal credits to compensate for 100% of annual residual emissions, while committing $100 million to high-durability removal offtakes by 2030 and a transition to durable removals for annual residual emissions by 2040. Salesforce also uses coalitions, nature initiatives, and its marketplace platform to broaden participation; Microsoft more visibly demonstrates how very large bilateral forward purchases can anchor new supply.

name: Salesforce
type: Corporate carbon buyer and market builder
description: Salesforce is a corporate carbon buyer and market-building climate actor. It purchases credits equivalent to annual residual scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions while separately reducing its own footprint, and plans to transition that annual compensation to durable removals by 2040. Its distinct ecosystem role combines procurement with coalitions that aggregate demand, longer-term commitments for engineered and nature-based removals, and marketplace infrastructure that exposes projects, prices, and ratings to more buyers. For developers, these channels can translate corporate commitments into clearer demand signals and contracted future revenue. Salesforce does not develop or certify projects; it influences which solutions scale through purchasing, quality expectations, and lower barriers to buyer participation.
overview: Salesforce sits on the demand side of the carbon ecosystem: it buys credits for its own residual emissions and works with other companies to create larger, clearer demand for high-integrity climate solutions. It also uses its software-platform capabilities to make project information, third-party ratings, pricing, and purchasing more accessible, but it is not a developer, registry, standard, implementation organization, or natural-capital fund manager.
representativeProjects: Annual carbon-credit portfolio — Salesforce purchases high-quality credits equivalent to the scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions it cannot yet reduce; its current portfolio mixes avoidance and removal, with a target to use only durable removals for annual residual emissions by 2040

Frontier — Salesforce joined the advance market commitment in 2024 with a $25 million commitment toward permanent carbon removal; its FY26 report identifies new portfolio deals with Planetary, Arbor Energy, and Reverion

Symbiosis Coalition — Salesforce, Google, Meta, and Microsoft collectively aim to contract for up to 20 million tonnes of high-quality nature-based removals by 2030; the coalition's first joint request for proposals focused on reforestation and agroforestry and streamlines access to multiple buyers

Net Zero Marketplace — a Salesforce Commerce Cloud marketplace that connects organizations with carbon-credit providers and publishes project information, pricing, and third-party ratings, illustrating how software infrastructure can broaden participation beyond large bilateral buyers

Blue carbon commitment — a goal to contract for 1 million tonnes of high-quality blue carbon credits by 2030, using buyer demand and market initiatives to support mangrove and other coastal-ecosystem solutions
standards: Credits issued under ICVCM Core Carbon Principles-eligible standards
Low climate-integrity risk rating from at least one independent carbon-credit ratings agency
Project assessment that also considers community rights, biodiversity, ecosystem health, adaptation, and resilience
Symbiosis Quality Pillars and pathway-specific criteria for nature-based removal procurement
Transparent project information, pricing, and third-party ratings in Net Zero Marketplace
financingApproach:
Salesforce primarily affects carbon finance by creating demand, not by lending to or developing projects. It purchases credits for annual residual-emissions compensation and has committed to contract for $100 million in high-durability carbon dioxide removal through long-term offtakes by 2030. A purchase commitment can give a supplier evidence of future revenue, but the supplier still raises and manages the separate capital needed to build and operate its project.

Coalitions amplify that mechanism. Frontier pools corporate commitments for permanent removal, while Symbiosis aggregates demand from Salesforce, Google, Meta, and Microsoft for nature-based removals. Symbiosis's joint diligence and request-for-proposals process gives developers one route to multiple potential buyers; long-term member offtakes and willingness to pay the cost of high-quality restoration are intended to reduce demand uncertainty and help qualifying projects attract financing.

Marketplace infrastructure works at a different scale. Net Zero Marketplace lowers search and participation costs by presenting projects, prices, and third-party ratings to organizations that may lack a large in-house procurement team. Together with buyer education and support for integrity criteria, that can build confidence and widen demand. These are indirect financing effects: Salesforce can improve revenue visibility and market access, but it is not thereby acting as a project lender, fund manager, registry, or developer.
businessModel: Salesforce is a software company whose relevant ecosystem role combines corporate carbon procurement with market-building. Corporate funds pay for credits and advance purchase commitments; coalitions aggregate its demand with that of peer buyers; and Salesforce's Commerce Cloud supports marketplace infrastructure connecting outside buyers with credit providers. The carbon-market activity supports Salesforce's climate strategy and platform ecosystem rather than making Salesforce a project developer, farmer aggregator, registry, carbon standard, implementation organization, or natural-capital fund manager.

comparison.platformComparison: Salesforce and Microsoft are both sophisticated corporate carbon buyers and members of the Symbiosis Coalition, but they make different market functions especially visible. Microsoft's program is known for very large, project-specific, long-term bilateral offtakes that can anchor individual suppliers and projects. Salesforce is more useful for understanding ecosystem building: it combines its own portfolio and durable-removal commitments with coalitions, aggregated demand, buyer education, quality advocacy, and marketplace infrastructure intended to bring more organizations into the market. Both create demand; Microsoft more visibly demonstrates bilateral scale, while Salesforce demonstrates how procurement can be paired with shared market infrastructure and collective action.

name: Nestlé
description: Nestlé is a multinational agrifood company that buys agricultural commodities at global scale and invests in agroforestry primarily to protect the long-term resilience of those supply chains. Through coffee, cocoa, dairy, and other sourcing programs, it funds farmer training, incentives, planting material, and supplier-led implementation that can improve yields, diversify income, restore soils and biodiversity, and reduce climate risk. Carbon is an enabling mechanism: emissions reductions and removals inside sourcing landscapes contribute to Nestlé's Scope 3 strategy, while stronger farms and more dependable supplies remain the central business case. Nestlé is therefore a supply-chain actor, not a carbon project developer or carbon-credit buyer first.
overview: Nestlé is a Switzerland-headquartered multinational agrifood company and major purchaser of coffee, cocoa, dairy, cereals, and other agricultural ingredients. Because almost all of its greenhouse gas emissions arise in Scope 3 and agriculture accounts for nearly two-thirds of its emissions, farm and landscape change is integral to both climate delivery and reliable sourcing. Nestlé uses regenerative agriculture, including agroforestry, to improve soil, water, biodiversity, farm productivity, livelihood resilience, and carbon storage. Its programs combine procurement, direct investment, supplier partnerships, agronomy, farmer incentives, and monitoring. The company reports in-chain reductions and removals toward its climate performance; it does not use offsets disconnected from its value chain for its net-zero target and says it has not converted its removal activities into credits sold on the voluntary market.
businessModel: Multinational agrifood and supply-chain company. Nestlé earns revenue from branded food, beverage, nutrition, and pet-care products and depends on long-term access to agricultural commodities. It uses responsible sourcing and regenerative agriculture programs to strengthen farm productivity and climate resilience, manage Scope 3 emissions and deforestation risk, and support farmer livelihoods. Agroforestry is one intervention within this sourcing model; carbon measurement supports climate accounting and investment decisions but is not Nestlé's primary product or business.
representativeProjects: Nescafé Plan 2030 — more than CHF 1 billion committed through 2030 to help coffee farmers adopt regenerative practices; in 2025 Nescafé sourced 53% of its green coffee from farmers adopting regenerative agriculture and supported more than 100,000 farmers in 15 countries through over 1,600 agronomists and field staff

Nestlé Cocoa Plan and Income Accelerator Program — cash incentives, in addition to certified-cocoa premiums, reward school enrollment, good agricultural practices, agroforestry, and income diversification among cocoa-farming families in Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana

Cargill and ETG | Beyond Beans cocoa projects — five-year agroforestry and reforestation initiatives designed to distribute more than two million shade trees with close to 20,000 farmers and deliver an estimated 500,000 metric tonnes of supply-chain emissions reductions and removals over 20 years

Nespresso AAA agroforestry with PUR and local partners — long-term coffee-landscape programs in six countries that use shade and native trees to protect soil and water, diversify farmer income, improve biodiversity, and stabilize coffee quality and production

Enterprise-wide regenerative agriculture transition — 27.6% of key ingredient volumes came from farmers adopting regenerative agriculture practices in 2025, spanning coffee, cocoa, dairy, grains, vegetables, and other priority supply chains

financingApproach:
Nestlé generally finances agroforestry as a supply-chain investment. Company and brand budgets support multi-year programs such as the Nescafé Plan 2030, while procurement relationships and supplier partnerships carry funding and implementation through traders, cooperatives, agronomists, and local organizations. Support can include seedlings, farm visits, training, monitoring, subsidized services, adapted purchasing, and direct investment in landscape projects.

Farmer economics are addressed through several mechanisms. Nescafé has piloted financial support for the costs and risks of transition; the Cocoa Plan offers certified-cocoa premiums; and the Income Accelerator makes traceable mobile-money payments for practices including agroforestry, in addition to those premiums. Technical assistance and income diversification help make tree-based systems useful before carbon value is realized.

Carbon strengthens the investment case but does not define it. Nestlé measures farm and landscape reductions and removals inside its value chain for Scope 3 and net-zero progress, using carbon-certification standards to support monitoring and verification. Its 2025 reporting states that it does not use out-of-value-chain offsets for its net-zero target and has not sold its removal activities as voluntary-market credits. Outside credits are limited to separate brand-level claims, so the dominant model is corporate and supplier financing for resilient sourcing rather than carbon-credit purchasing or project revenue.
ecosystemFit: Nestlé creates demand for agroforestry by embedding it in commodity procurement, regenerative agriculture programs, and Scope 3 delivery. It funds and coordinates suppliers and implementation partners but is not a carbon developer, registry, standard setter, MRV specialist, or natural-capital fund manager. Emissions reduction, in-chain removals, and insetting support a broader objective: resilient farms, farmer livelihoods, biodiversity, and dependable agricultural supply.

comparison.platformComparison: Nestlé and Mars are easily confused in this ecosystem because both fund agroforestry through commodity procurement, supplier programs, farmer support, and Scope 3 strategies rather than selling carbon credits. Nestlé demonstrates a broad, enterprise-wide regenerative agriculture portfolio across coffee, cocoa, dairy, and other ingredients, with brand programs, premiums, and direct farmer incentives. Mars offers a more concentrated teaching example around cocoa transformation, supplier co-investment, livelihoods, traceability, and blended partnerships, alongside a growing multi-crop portfolio. The lesson is not a different ecosystem role but a different strategic emphasis within agrifood supply-chain finance.

name: Mars
description: Mars is a multinational agrifood and pet-care company that buys agricultural commodities and invests in agroforestry and regenerative agriculture to make those supply chains more resilient. Its most concentrated example is cocoa: supplier programs combine traceability, forest protection, shade trees, agronomy, farmer-income support, and climate-smart production. Mars also funds regenerative practices in grains, rice, dairy, mint, and other ingredients. These interventions can reduce emissions and increase removals inside its value chain, but carbon is an enabling mechanism rather than the product. Mars is fundamentally a commodity purchaser and corporate sustainability actor, not a carbon project developer or carbon-credit buyer first.
overview: Mars is a United States-headquartered, family-owned multinational whose food, snacking, and pet-care businesses depend on agricultural ingredients. Raw materials dominate the climate footprint of its snacking business, and agricultural ingredients account for nearly 60% of its wider value-chain emissions, making farm-level change important to both dependable sourcing and its Scope 3 strategy. Mars works through suppliers, farmer organizations, governments, and implementation partners rather than owning most farms or developing carbon projects itself. In cocoa, its Cocoa for Generations strategy links responsible sourcing, traceability, farmer livelihoods, deforestation prevention, agroforestry, biodiversity, productivity, and climate resilience. Across other ingredients, climate-smart agriculture programs use locally appropriate practices such as cover crops, reduced tillage, crop diversification, lower-carbon inputs, and farmer training. Mars counts value-chain emissions reductions and carbon sequestration toward decarbonization; it treats high-quality removal credits outside the value chain as a later tool for residual emissions, not as the main purpose of its sourcing programs.
businessModel: Multinational agrifood, snacking, and pet-care company and purchaser of agricultural commodities. Mars earns revenue from consumer products, pet-care products and services, and veterinary businesses, not from developing or selling carbon credits. It invests in responsible sourcing and regenerative agriculture to secure future ingredient supply, improve productivity and farmer resilience, manage deforestation and human-rights risks, and reduce Scope 3 emissions. Agroforestry is especially relevant in cocoa, where shade and diversified tree systems can support yields, biodiversity, adaptation, household income, emissions reduction, and carbon removal at the same time.
representativeProjects: Cocoa for Generations — a $1 billion, 10-year strategy launched in 2018 that funds supplier and partner programs for a more modern, inclusive, and sustainable cocoa supply chain; its Responsibly Sourced Cocoa Program combines farm-level traceability, third-party verification, forest safeguards, and livelihood interventions

Mars and ofi cocoa collaboration in Ecuador — a five-year shared-supply-chain program whose first phase is expected to help more than 960 farmers adopt regenerative practices across more than 9,000 hectares, including transition from full-sun monoculture to multistrata agroforestry, lower-carbon fertilizer, crop-residue management, and biochar

ACTIVE in Indonesia — a $7.2 million Global Development Alliance with USAID and the Institute for Development Impact that advances diversified cocoa agroforestry, crop insurance and access to finance, farm productivity, income diversification, and community-led implementation in Sulawesi

Cocoa & Forests Initiative in Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana — Mars works through suppliers and the industry-government framework on farm mapping, deforestation-risk assessment, farmer training, forest protection, restoration, and shade-tree distribution; its 2024 cocoa report recorded 1.8 million non-cocoa trees distributed for on-farm planting

Enterprise climate-smart agriculture portfolio — approximately 77 projects across 26 countries and 12 crops in 2025, supporting Mars' ambition to help deliver more than one million acres of regenerative agriculture practices by 2030; projects span cocoa, grains, rice, dairy, mint, and other ingredients

financingApproach:
Mars finances agroforestry primarily as a supply-chain and business-resilience investment. Corporate sustainability, procurement, and brand budgets fund multi-year strategies such as the $1 billion Cocoa for Generations commitment and the Net Zero Roadmap's investment program. Capital and implementation typically flow through cocoa traders, ingredient suppliers, farmer organizations, NGOs, agronomy providers, and public-private partnerships; the 2025–2029 Ecuador collaboration with ofi, for example, is structured as co-investment in their shared cocoa supply chain.

Support reaching farmers can include training, planting material, farm rehabilitation, traceability systems, crop insurance, savings groups, access to finance, and payments or incentives for adopting practices. Mars supports cocoa certification and premium transparency, backs the Living Income Differential in Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana, and has used direct cash incentives in other commodities: its five-year Farmer Forward dairy program with Fonterra combines funded tools and technology with annual performance payments for participating farmers. Blended arrangements can add public or philanthropic capital, as in the $7.2 million ACTIVE alliance with USAID and I4DI.

Carbon value is secondary and takes two distinct forms. Within Mars' supply chains, avoided land-use emissions, lower farm emissions, and increased sequestration can contribute to Scope 3 and net-zero performance; some supplier programs also compensate farmers for measured carbon outcomes. Separately, Mars says it expects to use high-quality removal credits for residual emissions at net zero in 2050 and has invested in Livelihoods Carbon Fund 3 to build future supply. That external-credit strategy should not be confused with its dominant model: financing farm and supplier transitions to secure commodities, livelihoods, resilience, biodiversity, and in-chain climate benefits.
ecosystemFit: Mars creates demand and funding for agroforestry by embedding it in commodity sourcing, supplier programs, farmer-livelihood initiatives, and Scope 3 delivery. Suppliers and specialist partners perform much of the farm implementation, monitoring, certification, and landscape coordination; Mars is not a carbon project developer, registry, standard setter, MRV provider, or natural-capital fund manager. Carbon reductions, removals, and insetting-like outcomes support the broader commercial objective of resilient farms and reliable agricultural supply, while external removal credits are reserved for residual emissions rather than driving the sourcing model.
